description: Crew model for full-house residential deconstruction. Constants (on-site all day, break when they can) + shift workers (90/45 cadence). 6 shifts per day × 2 days = 12 total shifts.

philosophy.the45: The 45 is NOT a break — it's the other half of the job. 90 minutes of sustained physical work, then stop, step back, ask: What took too long? What broke? What's the better way? What did I learn about this house? Then go back in with the insight.
philosophy.compensation: ML Systems pays for both. 90 minutes of labor (the body) + 45 minutes of problem-solving (the brain). Both are compensated.
philosophy.2xClaim: Conventional crew: 8 hrs → 6 hrs effective output (fatigue, lunch, slowdowns, repeated mistakes). ML Systems: 6 shifts → 9 hrs focused labor + 4.5 hrs problem-solving that compounds. Each 90-min block is 15%+ more efficient than continuous fatigued labor. 2 days with this structure > 4 days conventional.
philosophy.flexibility: Nobody is required to work consecutive shifts. Pick the ones that fit your schedule or skills. Work 1 shift and go home, or work all 6 if you're built like that.

the45Feeds.why: Listings go live during the decon — not after
You touched it, you know its condition
Every house trains the model for the next house
Material specs for the design reference library

the45Feeds.activity: Help plan the next shift's work
the45Feeds.why: Problem-solve with Sal and the constants

phaseMapping.constantsDoing: Sal: supervise + lockout. Crane op: setup + rigging. Brokk op: saw-cutting foundation walls.
Sal: supervise. Crane op: finish setup, begin roof rigging. Brokk op: continues foundation.
Sal: on structure or ground. Crane op: in cab. Brokk op: core drilling.
Crane op: continuous. Brokk op: freeing panels.
Crane op: continuous. Brokk op: continues foundation.
Crane op: wrapping for day. Brokk op: may continue or wrap.
Crane op: in cab. Brokk op: foundation continues.
Crane op: lifts. Brokk op: panels fully freed.
Crane op: quick picks. Brokk op: in-hole, loosening slab.
Crane op: lifts. Brokk op: crushing footings.
Crane op: last lifts. Brokk op: done.
Sal: final walkthrough, BOH review. Crane: done or helping.

capacityCheck.vsConventional: 7 people × 14 hrs = 98 person-hours. This model: ~140 person-hours. 42% more capacity, spread so nobody burns out.
capacityCheck.craneTime: Shifts 3–6 (Day 1) + Shifts 7–10 (Day 2) = 8 shifts × 90 min = 12 hrs crane windows. Original needed ~12–14 hrs. Fits.
capacityCheck.foundationRunway: Brokk starts Shift 1 Day 1 (5:30 AM). Foundation panel lifts start Shift 9 Day 2 (10:00 AM). 28.5 hrs elapsed for Brokk prep before first foundation crane lift. Original had 4.5 hrs head start — this has 6× more runway.

fieldTestingNeeded: Do constants actually need fixed breaks, or do they naturally pause during rigging resets and shift transitions?
Is the universal 45-minute solve window too long? Too short? Maybe some shifts 30, some 60.
Can phases really fit within 90-minute labor blocks? Kitchen alone is 1.5–2.5 hrs — may spill into Shift 2.
Does the crane actually stop during break? Mid-rig = finish the lift, break starts after.
Right number of shifts per day? 6 might be too many, 5 might be enough. Field proves it.
Shift crew continuity — does it matter if different people show up? Constants as anchors may be enough.

constants.description: On-site 5:30 AM – 7:00 PM both days. Break when they can — not on a fixed schedule. Crane op rests during rigging reset. Brokk op pauses between cuts. The right rhythm comes from the field, not a document.

roles.name: Sal — Supervisor / Founder
responsibilities: Full-site structural awareness — knows what's been cut, rigged, and what's next
Safety authority — can halt any operation
Cross-shift synthesis — insights from one crew fed to next
Decision-making on material recovery (save vs discard)
Data capture — ontology insights from each project
During the 45: collects insights from crew — what took too long, what broke, what's the better way
roles.lucentLens: When the shift crew stops to think, Sal is the person they think WITH. A Shift 2 crew member notices trim pulls faster from the bottom — Sal carries that to Shift 3. The operation gets smarter every 2.25 hours. This is 'utilize the person, not the process' in practice.

roles.name: Crane Operator
responsibilities: Crane operation for all structural lifts
Load weight assessment and swing radius management
Weather monitoring (wind limits)
Communication with signal person
Rests during rigging resets — natural pause rhythm
roles.craneShifts: Active Shifts 3–6 (Day 1) + 7–10 (Day 2). Setup in Shifts 1–2. Done or helping ground by Shift 11–12.

roles.name: Brokk 170 Operator
responsibilities: Remote operation of Brokk demolition robot
Foundation wall saw-cutting (starts Shift 1 Day 1)
Core drilling lift holes
Panel freeing at footing joints
Footing crushing
28.5 hrs of prep runway before first foundation crane lift
roles.note: Starts Day 1 Shift 1 — 28.5 hrs elapsed before first foundation crane lift (Shift 9 Day 2). Done by Shift 11. Cross-trains ground roles off-phase.

shiftWorkers.description: 90 minutes of sustained physical labor + 45 minutes of problem-solving. Pick which shifts you show up for — nobody required to work consecutive shifts. Work 1 shift and go home, or work all 6.

roles.name: Rigger / Signal Person
responsibilities: Attach slings, shackles, spreader bars to loads
Verify center of gravity and load security
Give hand signals to crane operator (OSHA standard)
Manage tag lines during lifts

roles.name: Ground Lead
responsibilities: Lead ground disassembly of sections (reverse order)
Material quality assessment — recovery decisions
Tool selection for material preservation (flush-cut vs extract)
Staging zone assignments
During the 45: photograph materials, log conditions, plan next block

roles.name: Ground Support
responsibilities: Material carrying and staging
Sorting into recovery streams
Zone organization + cleanup
Nail sweeping (magnetic)
Soft strip (parallel with crane work)
During the 45: photograph what you just pulled for BOH, log data
roles.note: Entry-level role — feeds into ground lead and rigger with experience

minimum.note: Supervisor + crane op + Brokk op + 2 rotating ground

optimal.note: Rigger + cutter + 2 ground for structural shifts. 3 ground for soft strip and staging shifts.

peakShift.note: Shifts 3–5 (Day 1) and 7–8 (Day 2) are peak — crane active, full ground crew needed

payStructure.model: Day N = $N net (take-home). All crew except Sal.
payStructure.reviewGate: Day 365 — 4 criteria (Safety 30%, Skill 25%, The 45 Contribution 25%, Reliability 20%)
payStructure.payrollEngine: ontology/payroll-engine.json — full tax tables, compliance calendar, gross-up methodology

humanoidBridge.note: Every crew role maps to humanoid-ontology task primitives. Ground support = Tier 1 robot deployment (sweep, carry, sort). Ground lead = Tier 2–3 (structured, force-adaptive). Rigger + crane = Tier 4+ (complex spatial, safety-critical).

tier1_now: ground_support (partial) — sweep, carry, sort, magnetic sweep
tier2_near: ground_support (expanded) — organized stacking, material grading assist
robotDisplacement.lucentLens: Robot deployment follows Lucent Lens: does this reduce a human's hours? If yes, human must explicitly decide and approve. Logged.
